content.problemStatement:
Write a C++ program to:



Create a class Demo




Include:



A constructor that displays the message: "Object Created"
A destructor that displays the message: "Object Deleted"



Create an object in main() and observe the output.
content.inputFormat: First line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case contains no input.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print two lines: 'Object Created' and 'Object Deleted'.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The program creates an object whose constructor prints 'Object Created'. When the program ends, the destructor prints 'Object Deleted'.

sampleTestCases.output:
Object Created
Object Deleted
Object Created
Object Deleted
sampleTestCases.explanation: The program creates two objects, each triggering the constructor and destructor, resulting in the repeated output.

sampleTestCases.output:
Object Created
Object Deleted
Object Created
Object Deleted
Object Created
Object Deleted
sampleTestCases.explanation: The program creates three objects, each triggering the constructor and destructor, resulting in the repeated output.

solution.approach: The key insight here is to understand how constructors and destructors work in object-oriented programming. A constructor is a special method that is automatically called when an object of a class is created. Similarly, a destructor is a special method that is automatically called when an object of a class is about to be destroyed. In this problem, we create a class Demo with a constructor that prints 'Object Created' and a destructor that prints 'Object Deleted'. We then create an object of this class in the main function, which triggers the constructor and destructor, resulting in the desired output.
solution.timeComplexity: O(1) per test case, O(T) total.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) — the dominant structure is the object of the Demo class.
